questions.stem: Which best defines Artificial Intelligence?

options.A: A branch of mathematics focused on symbolic logic
options.B: The science and engineering of making machines that exhibit intelligent behaviour
options.C: A database system for large datasets
options.D: Software automating office tasks via macros

questions.explanation: Russell & Norvig (AIMA 4th ed.) define AI as 'the science and engineering of making intelligent machines', encompassing perception, reasoning, learning, and action.
questions.source: Russell & Norvig. AIMA 4th ed. MIT Press, 2020. https://aima.cs.berkeley.edu

questions.stem: Which framework organises AI definitions into a 2×2 matrix of thinking/acting vs human/rational?
questions.explanation: Russell & Norvig organise AI along two axes: thinking vs acting and human vs rational standard (AIMA Ch.1).
questions.source: Russell & Norvig. AIMA 4th ed. Ch.1. https://aima.cs.berkeley.edu

questions.stem: Why is the Turing Test an insufficient sole criterion for machine intelligence?

options.C: It is anthropocentric and can be passed by systems with no genuine understanding, as Searle's Chinese Room argues
options.D: It measures processing speed rather than knowledge

questions.explanation: Searle's Chinese Room (1980) shows a system may produce correct responses via syntactic rule-following with no semantic understanding. The test is also anthropocentric — human-like behaviour ≠ rational intelligence.
questions.source: Russell & Norvig. AIMA 4th ed. Ch.1; Turing. Computing Machinery and Intelligence. Mind, 1950.

questions.stem: How does narrow AI differ from AGI?

options.A: Narrow AI uses neural networks; AGI uses symbolic logic only
options.B: Narrow AI is task-specific and exists today; AGI replicates flexible human-level cognition and does not yet exist
options.C: Narrow AI runs on cloud servers; AGI on dedicated hardware
options.D: Narrow AI is supervised; AGI is always unsupervised

questions.explanation: Narrow AI excels at one domain (e.g., image classification, chess). AGI would match human cognitive flexibility across arbitrary tasks. As of 2026, AGI remains an open research goal.
questions.source: Russell & Norvig. AIMA 4th ed. Ch.1; Stanford HAI. https://hai.stanford.edu

questions.stem: ELIZA (1966) used pattern-matching substitution rules with no internal world model. Which AIMA definition does it best exemplify, and why does it fall short of rational standards?

options.A: Thinking humanly — models cognition via substitution
options.B: Acting humanly — mimics conversational output but has no reasoning or world model
options.D: Acting rationally — maximises a performance measure

questions.explanation: ELIZA produces human-like text (acting humanly) via substitution — no inference, no world model — failing rational standards. Weizenbaum's surprise at user credulity illustrates the gap between surface behaviour and genuine understanding.
questions.source: Russell & Norvig. AIMA 4th ed. Ch.1; MIT OCW 6.034. https://ocw.mit.edu/courses/6-034-artificial-intelligence-fall-2010

questions.stem: In which year was 'Artificial Intelligence' coined at the Dartmouth Conference?
questions.explanation: John McCarthy organised the Dartmouth Summer Research Project in 1956 where 'Artificial Intelligence' was formally proposed as a field name.
questions.source: Russell & Norvig. AIMA 4th ed. Ch.1. https://aima.cs.berkeley.edu

questions.stem: Which 2012 event launched the modern deep learning era?

options.B: AlexNet winning ImageNet LSVRC by a large margin

questions.explanation: AlexNet (Krizhevsky et al.) achieved top-5 error of 15.3% on ImageNet 2012 — ~10pp below the next competitor — catalysing the deep learning revolution.
questions.source: Krizhevsky et al. NeurIPS 2012. https://papers.nips.cc/paper/2012/hash/c399862d3b9d6b76c8436e924a68c45b-Abstract.html

questions.stem: What were the two principal causes of the AI Winters of the 1970s and 1980s?

options.A: Lack of programming languages and insufficient electricity
options.B: Overpromised results outpacing hardware, data, and algorithms, leading to funding withdrawal

questions.explanation: Both winters resulted from hype-disappointment cycles: expert systems were brittle; compute and data were insufficient. The Lighthill Report (1973) and DARPA cuts exemplify the pattern.
questions.source: Russell & Norvig. AIMA 4th ed. Ch.1. https://aima.cs.berkeley.edu

questions.stem: Why did expert systems like MYCIN fail to achieve widespread deployment despite impressive accuracy?

options.C: Knowledge-acquisition bottleneck, brittleness outside rule base, liability concerns, inability to learn from new data

questions.explanation: MYCIN achieved 65–70% diagnostic accuracy but was never deployed: knowledge-engineering bottleneck, brittleness, legal liability, and hospital integration barriers all prevented real-world use.
questions.source: Russell & Norvig. AIMA 4th ed. Ch.1. https://aima.cs.berkeley.edu

questions.stem: Classify AlphaFold 2 (2020) by: (a) AI paradigm, (b) learning type, (c) narrow vs general AI, with justification.

options.B: Deep learning; supervised; narrow AI — accurate at protein folding but cannot perform other cognitive tasks
options.C: Deep learning; reinforcement; AGI — surpasses human experts

questions.explanation: AlphaFold 2 uses a deep transformer trained on labelled protein sequences (supervised). It is narrow AI — superhuman at one task with no capability outside that domain. Surpassing experts in a single domain does not constitute AGI.
questions.source: Jumper et al. Nature, 2021. https://www.nature.com/articles/s41586-021-03819-2

questions.stem: Which correctly defines supervised learning?

options.A: Agent learns from rewards and penalties
options.B: Model learns f(X)→y from labelled input-output pairs
options.C: Model discovers hidden structure in unlabelled data
options.D: Model generates synthetic data by learning its distribution

questions.explanation: Supervised learning trains a function mapping inputs X to labels y using labelled (input, output) pairs. The label is provided for every training example.
questions.source: Russell & Norvig. AIMA 4th ed. Ch.19. https://aima.cs.berkeley.edu

questions.stem: GPT's pre-training on next-token prediction — generating its own labels from raw text — exemplifies which paradigm?
questions.explanation: Self-supervised learning generates supervision signals from the data itself (e.g., masking tokens or predicting the next token). No human-annotated labels are required.
questions.source: LeCun et al. Meta AI Blog, 2021. https://ai.facebook.com/blog/self-supervised-learning-the-dark-matter-of-intelligence

questions.stem: Which best distinguishes reinforcement learning from supervised learning?

options.B: In RL, an agent learns from delayed scalar rewards via trial and error; supervised learning trains on labelled pairs with immediate per-example feedback
options.C: Supervised is classification-only; RL is regression-only
options.D: RL requires no model; supervised always needs a neural network

questions.explanation: Key distinction: supervised receives a correct label per example (dense, immediate); RL receives a scalar reward that may be delayed and sparse, requiring exploration and credit assignment across action sequences.
questions.source: Sutton & Barto. RL: An Introduction 2nd ed. Ch.1. http://incompleteideas.net/book/the-book-2nd.html

questions.stem: A fraud-detection system is trained on 10M transactions labelled 'fraudulent' or 'legitimate'. Which paradigm and primary challenge apply?

options.C: Supervised classification — severe class imbalance (fraud is rare)

questions.explanation: Labelled data → supervised classification. Primary challenge: class imbalance — fraud <0.1% of transactions. A naïve model achieves 99.9% accuracy but zero fraud recall. Techniques: SMOTE, class-weighted loss, precision-recall AUC.
questions.source: James et al. ISLR 2nd ed. Ch.4. https://www.statlearning.com

questions.stem: You have 50,000 customer purchase records with no labels and want to segment customers for targeted marketing. Which paradigm and algorithm should you use?

options.A: Supervised — logistic regression for probability scores
options.C: Unsupervised — k-means clustering to discover natural groupings
options.D: Self-supervised — BERT fine-tuning for contextual embeddings

questions.explanation: No labels → supervised inapplicable. Goal is discovering structure → unsupervised. K-means partitions customers into k clusters minimising within-cluster variance. Choose k via elbow method or silhouette score.
questions.source: James et al. ISLR 2nd ed. Ch.10. https://www.statlearning.com

questions.stem: In PEAS, what does 'E' represent?
questions.explanation: PEAS: Performance measure, Environment, Actuators, Sensors. Environment = the external context the agent perceives and acts upon.
questions.source: Russell & Norvig. AIMA 4th ed. Ch.2. https://aima.cs.berkeley.edu

questions.stem: Which agent type maintains an internal state to handle partial observability?
questions.explanation: A model-based reflex agent maintains an internal world model for partially observable environments. Simple reflex agents react only to the current percept with no memory.
questions.source: Russell & Norvig. AIMA 4th ed. Ch.2. https://aima.cs.berkeley.edu

questions.stem: Why is a utility-based agent more flexible than a goal-based agent?

options.A: Utility agents learn faster via gradient descent
options.B: Utility agents handle trade-offs by assigning numeric utility to states; goal agents only distinguish goal from non-goal states
options.D: Utility agents operate in fully observable environments only

questions.explanation: Goal agents classify outcomes as goal/not-goal — binary. Utility agents assign real-valued utility, enabling nuanced trade-offs — e.g., a self-driving car balancing speed, safety, and comfort simultaneously.
questions.source: Russell & Norvig. AIMA 4th ed. Ch.2. https://aima.cs.berkeley.edu

questions.stem: A chess-playing agent knows the complete board state; each move has a deterministic outcome; decisions are sequential. How should this environment be characterised?
questions.explanation: Chess is fully observable (complete board visible), deterministic (certain outcomes), discrete (finite positions), sequential (each move affects future), adversarial (two opposing players).
questions.source: Russell & Norvig. AIMA 4th ed. Ch.2 & Ch.5. https://aima.cs.berkeley.edu

questions.stem: Design a PEAS specification for an AI medical diagnosis assistant. Which option is most complete and accurate?

options.A: P: speed; E: internet; A: keyboard; S: microphone
options.B: P: diagnostic accuracy, patient safety, explanation quality; E: EHRs, symptom inputs, lab results; A: diagnoses, confidence scores, referral flags; S: text/structured data from clinician or patient portal
options.C: P: revenue generated; E: billing system; A: invoice generator; S: insurance codes
options.D: P: patients seen; E: waiting room; A: scheduler; S: check-in kiosk

questions.explanation: Rigorous PEAS: Performance = accuracy, safety, explainability. Environment = EHRs, labs, imaging, symptoms. Actuators = ranked diagnoses, confidence intervals, referral triggers. Sensors = structured clinical data and NLP inputs from patient portals.
questions.source: Russell & Norvig. AIMA 4th ed. Ch.2; EU AI Act Annex III. https://eur-lex.europa.eu/legal-content/EN/TXT/?uri=CELEX:32024R1689
